preFix.title: Compressed italic hand
preFix.subtitle: The condensed form of italic, narrower letter widths, tighter spacing, for manuscripts and narrow-column work
preFix.excerpt: Compressed italic reduces the width of each letter body to approximately half the x-height rather than the standard two-thirds. The slope increases slightly and the interspace between letters tightens. The result reads as italic but fits more words per line, making it the natural choice for marginalia, narrow column formats, and closely-written manuscripts.
preFix.sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing & Illuminating & Lettering (1906), chapters on italic and condensed scripts. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john.

content.text: Standard formal italic is built on an o that is approximately two-thirds as wide as it is tall.
narrows that o to half the x-height, the

becomes a narrower oval rather than a broad ellipse. The same compression carries through every letter: n-arches are narrower, u-bowls tighter, the a and d more upright. The overall effect is a hand that reads as italic in rhythm and weight but takes up less horizontal space per word.
The compressed o as the reference letter

Rule a guide sheet at italic proportions: 5 nib widths x-height, nib angle 45°, slope 5-10°. Write a column of o letters with the standard two-thirds width. Beside it, write a column with the width reduced to half the x-height. Compare the
: the compressed o should look like a narrow oval rather than a broad one. This comparison is the anchor for the entire alphabet, if the o is right, the rest follows.
The letters group into families that share the same shape modifications:
Round group (o, c, e, d, g, q, b, p): narrow the oval to half x-height width.
Arch group (n, m, h, u, a): tighten the arch so it branches earlier, at one-third of the x-height rather than one-half.
Diagonal group (v, w, x, z, k): reduce the spread of the angles; the diagonals are steeper.
Straight group (i, l, t, r, f): no width change; these are already narrow. Increase slope by 2-3° so the straights harmonise with the tighter round and arch letters.

Compressed italic suits narrow column work: marginalia, index columns, long prose on A5 or smaller. It also works for formal quotations where standard italic would run too wide. Avoid it for display headings, where the narrow form reads as cramped rather than elegant.

items.fix: Raise the branching point to one-third of x-height as the letters narrow. Compression requires both narrowing the body and raising the branch.
items.cause: The width was reduced but the arch branching point was not adjusted, the arch still branches at mid-x-height, producing a stubby form.
items.symptom: The compressed letters look squashed rather than elegant

items.fix: Set 50% of x-height as the minimum. Below this, individual letters become indistinct. Even professional calligraphers rarely compress below 45%.
items.cause: Over-compression, letter width below 40% of x-height.
items.symptom: Words become unreadable at the compressed width

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
brand-trademark: brand name "Anchor" — generic equivalent is "salted butter"
